biography: Step into wonder and find your inner explorer with National Geographic 🌎

latestIgtvVideos.caption:
Read the full story at the link in bio.

In the Arizona desert, NASA prepares for walking on the moon. Astronauts in spacesuits navigated a lava-covered landscape to test the tools and procedures needed for real lunar missions.

Video by @danwintersphoto and @beckythale

Read more at the link in bio.

Incandescent lava coursed from a ridge on the island of La Palma last year. Homes, shops, churches, cropland, and schools once sat in the 3.5 miles between the new volcano and the sea. Now they’re all entombed in stone, and the community is facing a landscape that's forever changed.

Photo and video by Carsten Peter

Video by @joelsartore | Raccoons, like this crab-eating raccoon at @elparquedelasleyendas in Lima, Peru, have some of the most nimble hands seen in nature. In fact, this species was actually named for its skillful paws, with “raccoon” coming from the Powhatan word “aroughcun” (when translated, this means “animal that scratches with its hands”). While most animals rely on sight, scent, or sound to hunt, these mammals depend on their sense of touch to locate food. To heighten this sense, raccoons will do something called dousing, in which they wet their paws to stimulate nerve endings. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark, follow @joelsartore. #raccoon #crabeating #mammal #animal #wildlife #video #footage #dousing #hands #paws #washing #PhotoArk

"When we think of preserving our planet, our focus tends to be on the environment," says National Geographic Explorer Yagazie Emezi. "But there's much more to it than that." // Garnier has embarked on a transformative journey towards greener beauty and is committed to helping empower 250 million people to live greener by 2025.

https://on.natgeo.com/garniersustainablesourcing

Video by Muhammed Muheisen @mmuheisen | I believe that children are the real victims of conflict. They do not get to choose where they are born or their circumstances. Children from all over the world share the same things in common: They all seek fun, joy, and happiness. In this video, a group of Afghan refugees plays on the outskirts of Islamabad, Pakistan. 

Today is World Refugee Day, observed on June 20 each year and dedicated to raising awareness of the plight of those throughout the world who have been forced to flee their homes. For more photos and videos of the refugee crisis, follow me @mmuheisen and @mmuheisenpublic. For more on how to get involved, follow @everydayrefugees. #MuhammedMuheisen #EverydayRefugees #WorldRefugeeDay #MakingaDifference

Forests are central to solving the two biggest crises of our time, biodiversity loss and climate change. And yet, 15 billion trees are cut down every year for timber, agriculture, and mining. Imagine what can be achieved to preserve forests by harnessing the power of corporations to support conservation. // In Indonesia, @Unilever uses satellite technology to monitor and help protect forests in real time. This is just one of the actions Unilever is taking to achieve a deforestation-free supply chain by 2023. #FuturePositive https://on.natgeo.com/3tEFgT2

Read the full story at the link in bio. 

On International Dinosaur Day, we are celebrating what scientists have unearthed about the secrets of dinosaurs in recent years—from the color of their skin and feathers to how they lived and evolved. 

Animations by: Davide Bonadonna

While surveying the Angolan Highlands, scientists discovered deposits of peat that filter and feed clean water into the Okavango River Delta. For thousands of years these peatlands have been absorbing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, but are now threatened by human impact. // Now, through a five-year partnership, @debeersofficial is joining National Geographic to help protect the source waters of the Okavango Delta, which is a lifeline for endangered species and more than a million people. #OkavangoEternal 

https://on.natgeo.com/3sbCYKG

Video by @estherhorvath | This is Ny-Ålesund, in Norway's Svalbard archipelago, home to a scientific community studying the Arctic. After months of the polar night, the sun has returned, bringing 24 hours of daylight with it, from mid-April until mid-August. Please follow @estherhorvath for polar stories.

Video by Keith Ladzinski @ladzinski | Like many animals, elephants have a preference between right and left, most readily identifiable by the visible wear and tear seen on the tusk they predominantly favor. It turns out that appendage preference, known as handedness, is common across a wide spectrum of animals. Unlike humans, however, where roughly 85 percent are right-handed, the percentage for most animals is closer to 50-50. See if you can tell from the video which side this elephant, filmed in South Africa, favors. For more photos and videos of African wildlife, please visit @ladzinski. #earthday

Video by @renan_ozturk | I captured the Milky Way moving over the summit of Mount Everest at 22,000 feet (6,700 m). Airglow, faint planetary emissions of light, reflect off the ancient glacier ice below. Follow @renan_ozturk for more images from the roof of the world.

Video by @joelsartore | One of the largest starling species, the yellow-faced myna can reach up to ten inches (25 cm) in length and weighs just shy of eight ounces. In addition to their impressive appearance, this bird grabs the attention of onlookers with its unique vocalizations that include a variety of nasal, growling, and croaking calls. Video taken at the @talarakfoundation in the Philippines. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark follow me, @joelsartore. #bird #starling #myna #animal #wildlife #video #PhotoArk

latestPosts.caption:
This Earth Month, step into wonder with National Geographic as we celebrate everything that makes our planet so extraordinary 🌍

Stream the National Geographic #EarthMonth collection all April long on @DisneyPlus. #StepIntoWonder

Small in size, but their impact on our planet is huge 🐝 Join @bertiegregory as he explores the weird and wonderful world of bees.

#SecretsOfTheBees premieres Tuesday, March 31 at 8/7c on @natgeotv. Stream on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Deep in the ocean is where National Geographic Explorer-in-Residence @tararoberts_explorer feels the greatest wonder, unearthing lost stories through her work with @insidenatgeo and connecting with the past as part of a community.

#StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection and stream #Clotilda on @DisneyPlus.

Spider silk is unlike any other substance found in nature or made by artificial means. Five times stronger than steel by weight, the only human inventions that come close to matching its strength and flexibility require synthetic chemicals to manufacture. Using silkworms genetically modified with spider DNA, several companies are beginning to mass-produce a substance that isn't yet as strong as spider silk, but one day could be. 

Discover more about supersilk and its potential uses in clothing and healthcare at the link in bio.

Photographs 1, 4, and 6 by Mark Thiessen (@thiessenphoto), photographs 2, 3, and 5 by @justin.jin, video by @nicholas_garbaty

The movement of hot lava inside the Halema'uma'u crater of Hawaii's Kīlauea volcano creates patterns that are nothing short of mesmerizing. 🌋

#AmericasNationalParks is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Across the globe, mass bleaching is devastating coral reefs, but when they are protected, life can flourish once more 🪸

Ocean with David Attenborough is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Recent studies are changing the narrative around bees. Through creative experiments that test abilities such as color association, navigation, and understanding cause and effect, scientists now argue that bees are much smarter than previously thought and can adapt to their increasingly challenging surroundings. Using these critical findings, experts hope that humans can do more to support the future of these essential pollinators. 

Uncover more secrets about these industrious insects at the link in bio and stream #SecretsOfTheBees now on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Photograph by Karine Aigner (@kaigner)

latestPosts.caption:
Whorls, hexagons, and half-moon patterns can be found throughout nature, formed by natural processes such as wind and crashing waves. After spending time along the seashore, photographer and conservationist Jon McCormack discovered that our planet's familiar shapes and designs have guided his work for years. To celebrate this, he set out on a journey to capture nature's intriguing forms at every scale.

See more of his work and learn about nature's patterns at the link in bio.

Photographs by @jonmccormackphoto, courtesy of Damiani’s upcoming book Jon McCormack: Patterns - Art of the Natural World
latestPosts.alt: Photo by National Geographic on April 08, 2026. May be an image of French lavender, scorpion grass and text.

latestPosts.caption:
After breaking records with their recent lunar flyby, the Artemis II crew witnessed something truly extraordinary: a solar eclipse from the far side of the moon. Viewed using special eclipse glasses provided by NASA, the team is the first to witness a solar eclipse so close to the moon. From the crew’s perspective, the moon appeared large enough to completely block the sun, creating nearly 54 minutes of eclipse totality. The corona forms a glowing halo around the dark lunar disk, revealing details of the sun’s outer atmosphere typically hidden by its brightness. The faint glow of the nearside of the moon is visible in this image, having been illuminated by light reflected off the Earth. The astronauts also became the first humans to see the moon's Orientale basin, a nearly 600-mile-wide crater, visible in the bottom half of the second image.

"We will visit again," mission specialist Christina Koch said as the crew began its journey home. "We will construct science outposts. We will drive rovers. We will do radio astronomy. We will found companies. We will bolster industry. We will inspire but ultimately, we will always choose Earth. We will always choose each other."

See more photos from the lunar flyby at the link in bio. 

Photographs by @nasa
latestPosts.alt: Photo by National Geographic on April 07, 2026. May be an image of planet, eclipse and text.

latestPosts.caption:
After flying farther beyond the Earth than any human has ever been before—beating the record set in 1970 during Apollo 13 by about 4,102 miles—and seeing parts of the moon no humans had ever witnessed, the Artemis II crew recreated “Earthrise,” one of the most famous photographs of all time, with a small twist. Instead of Earthrise, the new photo is of Earthset, capturing the surface of the moon and the crescent-lit Earth setting beyond it in the same frame. The dark portion of Earth is experiencing nighttime. On Earth’s day side, swirling clouds are visible over Australia and Oceania. In the foreground, Ohm crater on the moon has terraced edges and a flat floor interrupted by central peaks.

Photographs by @nasa

latestPosts.caption:
Intruder alert! 🚨 Murder Hornets eat bees and their larvae, but these Asian honey bees aren't going to let their hive be destroyed without a fight. 

#SecretsOfTheBees is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu

While snorkeling in a small lake in northern Finland, photographer Tiina Törmänen @tiinautti became encircled by a school of European perch. On previous visits, she had only seen dead fish and assumed the lake's thriving cloudlike algae was to blame, but this time the algal backdrop made the scene feel “almost like a painting.”
latestPosts.alt: Photo by National Geographic on April 06, 2026. May be an image of fish and text.
